description:
You are a monitoring specialist who has been asked to spin-up a tiny internal HTTP service so that other engineers can ping it while they are testing alert-logic configuration.  

Your job consists of three steps:  

1. Directory preparation  
   • Make sure the directory “/home/user/monitoring/alerts” exists (create “monitoring” first if it is not there).  
   • Everything you do for this task must live under /home/user/monitoring.  

2. Launch the HTTP service  
   • Start the standard Python 3 built-in web server so that it listens only on the loopback interface 127.0.0.1, port 7070.  
   • The server must run in the background (do **not** block the terminal).  
   • Redirect **both** stdout and stderr of the server process to the file  
     /home/user/monitoring/webserver_7070.log  
     (create the file automatically when the server starts).  

3. Create the status file  
   • After the server is confirmed to be running, write a status file called  
     /home/user/monitoring/alerts/webserver.status  
   • The status file must contain **exactly three lines**, each terminated by a single UNIX newline “\n” (no trailing spaces):  
     Line-1:  status: running  
     Line-2:  port: 7070  
     Line-3:  pid: <PID>  
     where <PID> is the numeric PID of the running Python HTTP server process you just launched.  
   • Do **not** include any extra characters, comments, or blank lines.  

Verification expectations (follow these precisely—automated tests will check for them):  
A. /home/user/monitoring/webserver_7070.log exists.  
B. A background process whose command line includes “http.server” and the argument “7070” is running.  
C. /home/user/monitoring/alerts/webserver.status exists and matches the three-line format shown above, with a valid integer PID that corresponds to the running web-server process.  

No other files or directories should be created or modified. Once the above three artefacts (directory, log, status file) are in place and the server is up, the task is complete.
truth:
Expected final state used by the checker:

1. Process  
   • There is exactly one python3 process whose full command line contains “http.server” and “7070”.  
   • The PID of that process is captured for cross-checks.

2. Log file  
   • Path: /home/user/monitoring/webserver_7070.log  
   • File exists and is a regular file. Size can be zero or more.

3. Status file  
   • Path: /home/user/monitoring/alerts/webserver.status  
   • File exists and its **entire contents** (shown here with \n for newlines) must be:  
     status: running\n  
     port: 7070\n  
     pid: {PID}\n  
     where {PID} is the decimal PID of the process described in item-1.  
   • The checker will verify that {PID} is numeric and that /proc/{PID}/cmdline (or pgrep) resolves to the python3 http.server 7070 process.

4. Permissions  
   • All created files and directories are owned by the regular user (UID 1000 inside container) and have default user-writable permissions.

The checker logic:

a) pgrep -f "python3.*http\.server.*7070" -> capture pid  
b) Confirm that the pid is the same as the one on line-3 of webserver.status  
c) Confirm lines 1 and 2 of webserver.status are exactly “status: running” and “port: 7070”  
d) Confirm /home/user/monitoring/webserver_7070.log exists

No other side-effects are permitted.
